title: AY20-21 Library Program Review Annual Update - Dec2020 (Part 1)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
1
Program: Library Date: December 15, 2020
Faculty Completing Form: Jude Baldwin
1. Describe annual progress / changes / events in the program.
The Library has experienced several major changes since submitting our Comprehensive Program
Review report (Jan/Feb 2020). These include:
• Going live with the new Library Services Platform (LSP) software, Alma/Primo
• Adapting services and operations to the coronavirus pandemic
• The retirement of Marcia Eblen (June 2020) and imminent retirement of Linda Kiely (January
2021)
The Library did a slow but successful rollout of Alma/Primo in January 2020. Switching to the LSP took a
year of work by all library staff but will save the college over $10,000 annually and offer students a
better searching experience using the same software as the CSU system, SOU, and OIT, as well as many
other colleges. The Community College Library consortium worked to gain ongoing funding for the LSP
at the state level but due to the coronavirus pandemic and resulting state budget shortfalls, the effort
was not successful. Since state funding had not been guaranteed, the college had planned for this
scenario. If the LSP is ever funded at the state level, it will of course save the college even more than the
$10,000! The downside of the LSP has been the constant maintenance and updates needed to keep this
complex system running optimally. It has created additional workload for staff who are already
overloaded.
The coronavirus pandemic impacted all campus operations, including the Library. Some Library
adjustments for Spring 2020 include:
• creating a Canvas site with information for students (having it in Canvas allowed us to update it
more easily as things frequently changed)
• creating a Library services overview video
• helping connect students with textbooks using free vendor platforms and scanning physical
books
• waiving all fines and extending due dates until the end of the semester
• visiting with classes via Zoom
• adding Zoom drop-in hours for both students and faculty for research help and questions about
textbooks or any library services
• adding content to the Services to Faculty site regarding free vendor resources, copyright
information, and textbook resources
Over the summer we added to the above list of Spring adjustments, making arrangements for an
anticipated mostly-online semester by:
• Adding texting as a contact method (Zipwhip subscription)

-- 1 of 6 --

title: AY20-21 Library Program Review Annual Update - Dec2020 (Part 2)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
2
• Purchasing e-textbooks whenever possible and emailing ebook links to each faculty member to
share with students (free access to vendor e-textbook sites ended in June)
• Arranging for free mailing services for physical materials to any student who wanted it
Over Summer 2020, the Library also developed a reopening plan for our physical space. This plan took
into consideration state, local, and campus guidelines, as well as library best practices (as they became
available).
The Library spent a lot of extra time in the Fall assisting with textbook access for students in need and
supporting faculty with special requests such as streaming films and audiobooks. Many of these
requests involved research into appropriate copyright practices. Since most students were not able to
come to the Library in-person, staff spent a lot of time creating videos and other materials to share
information with students, such as a video overview of Fall Library services and a how-to video on
finding reserve textbooks in the library catalog. We also created a promotional video for High School
Preview Day that gives a very general overview of what the COS Library has to offer incoming students,
which we hope can also be used in online SOAR sessions.
The Library was physically open Mondays and Wednesdays from 8:00 – 2:00. As of November 20, 2020
(before the Thanksgiving break), the Library had 103 in-person visitors, many of them repeat visitors.
While most were students, 10 were community members or COS employees. Most student visits were
brief: of the 72 students we have length-of-visit information for, 59 students (82%) stayed for less than
one hour for an average of only 15 minutes. Of the thirteen students who stayed for longer than an
hour, most wanted computers (4), study/wifi access (3), books/textbooks (3), research (1), other (2: they
were in and out of the Library waiting for the student doing research). Overall, the primary reason for
visiting the Library was for books/textbooks, with many students coming in for materials for their Oral
Interpretation (COMS 1500) and Fundamentals of Communication (COMS 1600) classes.
Reason for Library visit:
• Books/textbooks: 39
• Printing: 19
• Computer use: 21
• Other: 8
• Study/wifi: 7
• Not recorded: 6
• Research: 4
While our physically open hours were greatly reduced, they served us well with only two staff members
and the uncertainties of quarantines, days off, etc. The Academic Success Center (ASC) was open 8:00 –
2:00 on Tuesdays and Thursdays, complementing our hours. The Library frequently left our door ajar at
other times and many students used our resources during times when we were not officially open (late
afternoons or on Tuesday/Thursday/Friday).

-- 2 of 6 --

title: AY20-21 Library Program Review Annual Update - Dec2020 (Part 3)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
3
In addition to our physically open hours, the Library also offered “online service hours” when we were
available to help students by text, phone, email, or Zoom (drop-in hours and appointments) with
questions ranging from open hours and textbook access to citations and research assistance. The Library
has offered help by phone and email for many years but students rarely take advantage of this
opportunity. The current pandemic did not change that: very few students took advantage of any of
these contact methods.
While more faculty took advantage of the Library’s Canvas research modules, very few faculty took
advantage of the offer to include a librarian in their classroom to provide custom support materials, to
be available using Canvas email, or other methods. The two exceptions were Fundamentals of
Communication (COMS 1600), which had a librarian visit by synchronous Zoom, and College
Composition (ENGL 1001) with Liz Carlyle, where a librarian created videos with the instructor and on
specific topics, participated in discussion forums, was available through the Canvas email, and where the
instructor gave extra credit to those who contacted the Library for research help. These interactions
generated many student visits to the library and some requests for research assistance. Participating in
the discussion forum by replying with research tips to every student post was more time consuming
than a hands-on class session; end-of-the-semester assessment will show whether this time was helpful
to students.
In the Spring, the Library needs to work on new ways to reach students to make sure they know that
research help is available and to encourage them to use it. We will also conduct more faculty outreach.
In June 2020, Marcia Eblen retired. She had worked in the COS Media Center and the Library since 1975
when she was a COS student. Her expertise regarding our media collection and the Mount Shasta
Collection will be sorely missed, as will her institutional knowledge, and her talents with cataloging, the
budget, and displays. Due to campus budget shortfalls, originally it was thought that her position would
be rehired for Spring 2021 but that has been put off. Linda Kiely, the Library’s other Library Technician,
will be retiring in early January 2021. Linda’s calm, her willingness to cover the service desk, her caring
management of our student employees, and her general cheerfulness will also be sorely missed. As of
this time, the college will not rehire either Library Technician position until Summer 2021.
In the midst of a major software migration, a global pandemic, and staff shortfalls, the Library has still
managed to make progress on some of its goals identified in our AY19-20 Comprehensive Program
Review 4-Year Action Plan.
Progress on 4-Year Action Plan goals:
a) The Library will have adequate staffing to meet our workload needs and provide adequate open
hours.
• Due to college budget difficulties, having “adequate” staffing does not seem possible in
the near future. The Library is working with the Instruction Office to re-write the Library
Technician job description. Since only one position will be filled in the near term, we are
taking this opportunity to revise and reclassify the position to a higher pay grade so that
they can take on some of the administrative responsibilities of the LSP software. The

title: AY20-21 Library Program Review Annual Update - Dec2020 (Part 4)
text:
-- 3 of 6 --

College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
4
Library will function with only the Faculty Librarian position in Winter and Spring 2021
with the intention of having the new Library Technician start in early July 2021.
Functioning with so few staff will affect our ability to offer services and physical open
hours. The exact impact on which services will need to be scaled back or suspended
during this time are still being determined. The Library will function mostly or fully
online for Spring 2021. The Librarian will be hired to work extra days in January to keep
up with the additional workload and provide services to Winter Session students.
b) The Library will have increased instructional activities for students, including offering a credit-
bearing Research Skills and/or Information Literacy course
• The Library reviewed its video tutorials and handouts page. More materials have been
created and are in the process of being added. The page is being redesigned to be more
user-friendly, which will be especially important as the quantity of resources goes up.
These resources are also being integrated into the Articles & Databases page so that
students know they are available at a potential point of need.
• The curriculum for a 1-credit class, Library Research Information Literacy (LIBR 1001),
was submitted in September 2020.
• Linda Kiely, our student employee supervisor, completed Canvas training in September
2020.
• The Library investigated EZproxy Analytics: it has the data we would like regarding use
of our online collections but the price tag is not reasonable for our budget
($6,180/year).
c) The Library will have greater student engagement from developing a student-friendly space,
from adapting to the ways students use technology, from increased activities, and through
access to resources that meets student needs
• The Library was able to purchase some new furniture, specifically a couch and more
chairs. Price quotes for painting were obtained but may be out of our price range.
• LRC 9 is being slowly emptied so that it can serve as a multipurpose technology room.
Progress is slow since we have to make space elsewhere in the Library to relocate
materials. We hope to have it completed by the end of Spring 2021.
• The Library was able to add texting as a contact method in July 2020.
d) Increase faculty engagement with the library
• The Library discussed turning LRC 9 into a multipurpose room specifically for a faculty
media production space with Maria Fernandez, DL Coordinator, but it was not suitable
for that need. We are working towards making it a general, flexible, multipurpose space
instead.
• The Library has created additional instructional materials and has curated a collection of
similar materials created by others for faculty to use with their students. Content is
being housed on our Services to Faculty site.

-- 4 of 6 --

title: AY20-21 Library Program Review Annual Update - Dec2020 (Part 5)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
5
• The Library offered an overview of the new LSP to faculty during the Spring mandatory
Flex Day (May 22, 2020).
e) Improve our collections
• The Library’s Collection Development Policy was rolled into the creation of AP4040 (a
new AP for our campus); it was approved December 11, 2020. This version of the policy
is more general than originally written but provides the needed collection guidance,
including a challenge policy.
2. Describe progress on assessing SLOs and PLOs for the program.
The Library uses the most recent Institutional Set Standards as agreed on by the Academic Senate
when determining if our SLOs have been met:
• Transfer courses (such as ENGL 1001): ≥67%
• Credit courses (such as ENGL 0900): ≥67%
• Basic Skills courses (such as ENGL 0850): ≥45%
• Vocational courses: ≥70%
Library SLO #1: Students who receive formal research instruction provided by the COS Library will be able
to effectively locate needed information using a variety of library resources.
This SLO is evaluated primarily by post-session evaluations; in-class worksheets and activities are also
used when possible. Evaluation questions tied to this SLO ask students to rate themselves on the
following statements:
• The Library visit helped me to feel confident that I can get to the Library web site and databases
page
• The Library visit helped me to feel comfortable finding articles in EBSCO (e.g. Academic Search,
MasterFILE, etc.)
• The Library visit helped me to feel confident choosing keywords for a topic
Students consistently report that they are confident with these topics at the end of the session. For
example, after Librarian visits to Liz Carlyle’s three ENGL 1001 classes in Fall 2019 (note: no post-class
surveys were collected in Spring 2020 due to the coronavirus pandemic):
• 90% felt confident about getting to the Library web site and databases page
• 97% felt comfortable finding articles in EBSCO (e.g. Academic Search, MasterFILE, etc.)
• 78% felt confident choosing keywords for a topic
On the Library’s Spring 2020 annual Student Survey, 87% of respondents reported that “The library
provides information and instruction for students to help them develop their research skills (Consider
things like staff assistance, information handouts, the library web site & classroom instruction)” (rated
as Strongly Agree or Agree on survey).

-- 5 of 6 --

title: AY20-21 Library Program Review Annual Update - Dec2020 (Part 6)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
6
Library SLO #2: Students who participate in formal research instruction will be able to evaluate for
credibility information provided by the library, Internet resources, and other information sources.
This was not a topic the Librarian was asked to cover in class visits therefore the Library did not have the
opportunity to support or assess this SLO in formal instruction sessions.
Library SLO #3: Students who use the COS Library and utilize its resources, services, and formal
instruction will appreciate the value of libraries in supporting academic and/or career success and in
supporting life-long learning.
On the Library’s 2018 annual Student Survey, 91% percent of respondents reported that “Having used
the COS Library’s resources, services, and instruction, I appreciate the value of libraries in supporting
student success” (rated as Strongly Agree or Agree on survey). The 2020 Survey was collected entirely
online due to the coronavirus pandemic (it is normally collected at various campus locations as well as
online).
3. Discuss any support or obstacles encountered by the program.
The Library has been fortunate to maintain a status quo budget during tough budget times. Some of this
may be due to the fact that the Library saved the college over $10,000 by migrating to the LSP. We may
not be able to maintain our budget level, however, if the college budget continues to be strained.
The Library’s primary current challenges are:
• Staff shortages due to recent retirements and delays in rehiring positions
• Need for classified staff to participate in management of the Library’s LSP software (will require
reclassification of one position)
• Engaging students when everything is online and encouraging them to take advantage of Library
services
• Lack of granular data about online students’ use of our online collections so that we can make
better choices regarding content and outreach
4. Describe program budgetary needs or implications. (Attach completed CQIP form.)
The Library has several budgetary needs that have carried over from last year:
• Student Chromebooks for taking online proctored tests in the Library
• Laptop & projector for movie nights and other events in the Library Lounge
• New database to improve science coverage in our collection
The Library has been experiencing staffing challenges for many years. This year’s retirements of all
classified staff has increased those challenges to a new level.
• Reclassifying one Library Technician position from Range 114 to 117
• Increase in training budget for anticipated new library employees
• Funding for librarian adjunct hours

-- 6 of 6 --
